summary: Gruber argues that the real question about a rumored lower-cost iPhone 5C isn't whether Apple needs a cheaper iPhone—they've sold older models at lower prices for years—but why they'd shift strategy now. He identifies two key factors: unifying the lineup around Lightning ports and 16:9 displays by retiring the iPhone 4/4S, and pushing prices lower for non-subsidized markets where Apple currently can't compete. Using iPod Touch pricing as a floor, he estimates a 5C could sell for $349-$399 unsubsidized. He strongly argues that Apple must not cripple the 5C's software, insisting the premium models should differentiate on hardware alone.
key_points: The strategic shift isn't about offering a cheap iPhone (Apple already does that) but about replacing old high-end models with a purpose-built lower-cost device
Retiring the iPhone 4/4S would unify Apple's lineup around Lightning ports and 16:9 displays, eliminating legacy hardware
Apple needs to push the low end lower to compete in non-subsidized international markets where the unsubsidized iPhone 4 is still too expensive
iPod Touch pricing suggests a floor of $349-$399 for a cellular-capable lower-cost iPhone
Crippling software on the 5C (like removing Siri) would be a red flag for post-Jobs Apple—differentiation should come from hardware, not artificial software restrictions
money_quotes: The question to ask is not 'Does Apple need a lower-cost iPhone?', but rather, 'Why would Apple change its lower-cost iPhone strategy from selling years-old high-end models to a new model designed from the get-go to sell at lower prices?'
Take an iPod Touch and add cellular networking components. Boom, there's your lower-priced iPhone.
Modern-era Apple has never been afraid of cannibalizing its own products.
What I'd consider a red flag, a sign that perhaps we should start being worried about post-Jobs Apple, would be if Apple crippled the software on the 5C to diminish it against the 5 and 5S.
Apple can withhold cutting edge software features from old devices; they can't do that for brand-new ones.
There can be no penalty for buying a lower-priced iPhone 5C, only rewards for splurging on the higher-priced models.

The 5C is not about selling a piece of junk to some sort of unwashed masses; it's about continuing to push the price down to expand the iPhone's market without changing what the iPhone brand stands for.
If Apple thinks the iPhone 5S needs 'protection', then the 5S (and Apple itself) has problems.
key_insight: A lower-cost iPhone should expand the market by differentiating on hardware specs alone, never by artificially crippling software—because if the premium model needs protection from the cheap one, Apple has already lost.
